Raymond J. Starr, "Vergil's Seventh Eclogue and Its Readers:
Biographical Allegory as an Interpretative Strategy in Antiquity and Late Antiquity," CP 90 (1995) 129-138.
Arnold detects in Vergil's puer the first articulated consciousness of a peculiarly Roman program of poetry. Dix suggests that the hyacinth grows in the
Grynaean Grove, and that Damoetas' riddle concerns the shield of Ajax which
reflects the form of the heavens. Ruiz de Elvira supports qui . . . parentes as the
only acceptable reading, accepted by Juan Luis de la Cerda centuries ago. Starr's
article sheds light on antique practices in the interpretation of Vergil's opera .

J. Soubiran, "Entendre Virgile {Georg. IV 467-503)," Vita Latina
135 (1994) 15-23.
Horsfall read the manuscript and provided the introduction for Biotti 's
Geòrgie IV. De Grummond studies the humanizing factor in Vergil's plough.
Dyson examines Vergil's diatribe against urban life as an extended ingenious double entendre referring to the themes and content of epic poetry. The Temple's
Shadow alludes to Georgics 3.10-48, the future poem Vergil will construct for
Augustus. Kraggerud reviews the meaning and contextual function of Georgics
1.83: inaratus is past participle of inarare , "ploughed" cultus should be understood
more generally as "beautiful quality," "refined condition." Soubiran provides
stylistic and metrical notes.

Heidi В. Vierow, Narrative Voices in the Aeneid. Diss., Duke U.,
1994.
Goldberg treats epic in its cultural context that encouraged poets to develop
and Roman society to value a Latin epic tradition. Lesueur studies the Aristaeus
epyllion as an adumbration of the Aeneid. Lyne indicts the fallacy of audience
limitation in approaching classical texts; his remarks on intertextuality embrace
Vergil, Aen. 6.458-460, 851, and 4.704f. McKay's НЕС address highlights the
storm-center of fusion or confusion in Latin studies ... in Vergilian scholarship.
Martindale (57) fires salvoes at the 20th-century obsession with ambiguity in
Vergil. O'Hara argues that Vergil provides an "inconsistent, indeterminate
portrait" of Italy and the Italians in Aeneid 7-12, and explores contradictory
aspects of the associations of the Trojan/Italian sides in the war with the
Gigantomachy myth. He closes with reflections on the potential strengths and
weaknesses of a critical approach that tries to interpret, rather than explain away,
inconsistencies. Thomas's article expands the zodiacal arguments of Godefroid de
Callatay (45).
AENEID: INDIVIDUAL BOOKS.
